gold_final_answer: a. No such function exists. b. Yes, such a function exists.

(Z/17)* is cyclic of order 16. Homomorphisms to Z/8 correspond to choosing image of generator g: must have order dividing gcd(16,8)=8. So image is subgroup of Z/8 of order dividing 16, any element works since 8|16... wait, we need f(g)∈Z/8 with 16·f(g)≡0 mod 8, always true. So any homomorphism from Z/16 to Z/8 exists.

The image of a is determined by discrete log. Let g be primitive root mod 17, say g=3. Then 3 has order 16.

f(3)=1: need homomorphism sending 3→1 mod 8. Since 3 generates, f(g^k)=k mod 8. Check: 3^16=1, f maps to 16 mod 8 = 0. ✓. So f(3)=1 exists.

f(2)=1: 2 = 3^k mod 17 for some k. 3^1=3, 3^2=9, 3^3=27=10, 3^4=30=13, 3^5=39=5, 3^6=15, 3^7=45=11, 3^8=33=16=-1, 3^9=-3=14, 3^10=-9=8, 3^11=-10=7, 3^12=-13=4, 3^13=-5=12, 3^14=-15=2. So 2=3^14. Then f(2) = 14·f(3) mod 8. For f(2)=1, need 14t ≡1 mod 8, i.e., 6t≡1 mod 8. gcd(6,8)=2, doesn't divide 1. No solution.

For x≡0 mod 17, f(x) can be anything? Actually f(0): 0=0·y, but 0 is divisible by 17 so condition (i) doesn't apply when x or y divisible by 17. Actually condition says "x,y not divisible by 17". So f on multiples of 17 is unconstrained (except periodicity). Fine.

So for nonzero residues mod 17, f restricted to (Z/17)* must be homomorphism to Z/8. So answer: (a) No, (b) Yes.

So (a) no, (b) yes.

**Setup.** By condition (ii), $f$ depends only on residue mod 17. By (i), $f$ restricted to units mod 17 gives a homomorphism $\bar f: (\mathbb{Z}/17)^\times \to \mathbb{Z}/8$. The group $(\mathbb{Z}/17)^\times$ is cyclic of order 16; take generator $g=3$.

Computing powers of 3 mod 17: $3^1=3, 3^2=9, 3^3=10, \ldots, 3^{14}=2$. So $2 \equiv 3^{14} \pmod{17}$.

If $\bar f(3) = t$, then $\bar f(2) = 14t \equiv 6t \pmod 8$.

**Part (a): $f(2)=1$.** We'd need $6t \equiv 1 \pmod 8$. Since $\gcd(6,8)=2 \nmid 1$, no solution exists. So **no** such loggy function exists.

**Part (b): $f(3)=1$.** Define $\bar f(3^k) = k \pmod 8$. Since $3^{16}=1$ maps to $16 \equiv 0 \pmod 8$, this is a well-defined homomorphism $(\mathbb{Z}/17)^\times \to \mathbb{Z}/8$. Extend to $f:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}$ by setting $f(n)$ equal to this value on residues $1,\ldots,16 \pmod{17}$ and $f(n)=0$ when $17\mid n$. Conditions (i) and (ii) hold, and $f(3)=1$. So **yes**.

Final answer: (a) No; (b) Yes.
